coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of P-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Preethi, M.
Viswanathan, C.
Ponpandian, N.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), were gaining traction as promising optical detection materials due to its unique properties.In this research, rice stock is utilized as source of carbon and phosphorus is utilized as source of phosphorus to render phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots.The P-CQDs may be generated in an economical and environmentally responsible manner using this technique.Under optimal conditions of temperature and pH, the synthesized green fluorescent P-CQDs were monodispersed and spherical in shape having an average size of 5.4 nm and a high quantum yield of 32.61%. Because of the interaction of P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence properties of P-CQDs are quenched when they are in the presence of Cu2+ ions.With the incorporation of Cu2+ ions into P-CQDs, electron transport was enhanced, which may lead to fluorescence quenching, under optimal circumstances.According to the results of a spectroscopic analysis, Cu2+ ions can be detected at concentrations ranging from 0 to 260 μM, with a lower limit of detection of 0.032 μM.A real implementation of detecting Cu2+ in tap water was also carried out, and the results showed a satisfactory recovery rate. In a direct result of these, the implementation of a Cu2+ sensor may also be considered a real scenario.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rice stock derived P-CQDs
Stirrer-assisted technique
Non-fluorescence of the complex between CQD and Cu2+ ions
Cu2+ detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation on morphological and structural analysis of P-CQDs
Investigation on optical properties of P-CQDs
Fluorescence mechanism of P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, DST-PURSE, UGC-SAP, Government of India for the instrumental facilities provided through these programs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic view for discussing the work to identify in developing P-CQDs from rice stock and exhibiting the PL sensing of Cu2+ ions.

(a) The particle sizes of P-CQDs are expected to be approximately 5.4 nm, with spherical particles in size. (b) The histogram of P-CQDs particle diameter vs the area of particle size distribution obtained using Image J software was observed to be 5.4 nm in diameter.

XPS spectra for P-CQDs. (a) XPS spectral survey for P-CQDs. (b) A deconvoluted representation of the P2p spectrum for P-CQDs. (c) A deconvoluted representation of the C1 s spectrum for P-CQDs. (d) A deconvoluted representation of the O1 s spectrum for P-CQDs.

(a) In XRD pattern, there were two noticeable diffraction peaks about 14.4° (d001 =0.613 nm) and 27.5° (d002 =0.323 nm) which indicate that an amorphous structure exists in P-CQDs. (b) FTIR data reveal that the surface of P-CQDs is functionalized with hydrophilic groups, which renders them extremely soluble in water. (c) In the Raman spectra, P-CQDs may be identified by two different peaks: The D band reaches its maximum was 1372 cm−1, whereas its G band reaches its maximum was 1592 cm−1 and defect ratio for P-CQDs was 0.98, indicating the structural faults were related with amorphous phase. (d) An absorption peaks for P-CQDs reveals two prominent peaks on 256 nm and 487 nm, which corresponds to π-π * transition of conjugated CC bond and the n-π * transition of CO bond.

(a) The peak intensity for emission shifts depending on the wavelength of the associated excitation, which can be ranging from 310 to 430 nm; the maximum PL intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm with the emission wavelength of 535 nm. (b) The effect of concentration of carbon source to the phosphoric acid on PL intensity shows the maximum PL intensity was 3:3 because the concentration of phosphorous source was equal to that of carbon source due to surface passivation. (c) The effect of pH on the PL intensity for P-CQDs was altered from 3 to 11, the PL intensity for P-CQDs remained unchanged.This suggests that PL intensity of P-CQDs in acid and alkali solutions changed only minimally. (d) The effect of temperature on PL intensity was ranging from 30 °C to 80 °Creveals that the maximum PL intensity was 60 °C. (e) The emission wavelength does not change in response to changes in temperature and it can be used for a variety of applications in a multitude of environments. (e) The P-CQD dispersion stability remained unchanged after one month, indicating that no precipitation or significant colour change occurred in the solution.
Under UV irradiation, fluorescence photograph for P-CQDs with different metal ions at concentration of 260 µM were recorded (365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of P-CQDs at varied Cu2+ concentrations ranging of 0–260 µM. As Cu2+concentration increases, the PL intensity decreases, indicating Cu2+ ions can quench the P-CQD's fluorescence. (b) Under ideal conditions, there has been a perfect linear relationship between the concentrations of Cu2+ ions and the efficiency of quenching. The limit of detection of Cu2+ ions that could be found was 0.032 μM. (c) The Cu2+ ions had minimum quenching ratio when compared to other metal ions and this shows the detection of Cu2+ions.

(a) An absorption spectra for Cu2+ ions demonstrate the existenceof a significant absorption peak located around 245 nm. According to this, it appears that this would not overlap with an emission wavelength of 535 nm for P-CQDs. (b) The UV–vis spectra of P-CQDs demonstrate an absorption band located around 245 nm and this would not overlap with an emission wavelength of 535 nm for P-CQDs. (c) The covalent binding effect between P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions was demonstrated by the red shift of the absorbance band of P-CQDs– Cu2+.
The analytical performance of the proposed approach are being compared with the detection of Cu2+ ions.
Recovery of tap water in Cu2+ ions (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of P-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), were gaining traction as promising optical detection materials due to its unique properties.In this research, rice stock is utilized as source of carbon and phosphorus is utilized as source of phosphorus to render phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots.The P-CQDs may be generated in an economical and environmentally responsible manner using this technique.Under optimal conditions of temperature and pH, the synthesized green fluorescent P-CQDs were monodispersed and spherical in shape having an average size of 5.4 nm and a high quantum yield of 32.61%. Because of the interaction of P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence properties of P-CQDs are quenched when they are in the presence of Cu2+ ions.With the incorporation of Cu2+ ions into P-CQDs, electron transport was enhanced, which may lead to fluorescence quenching, under optimal circumstances.According to the results of a spectroscopic analysis, Cu2+ ions can be detected at concentrations ranging from 0 to 260 μM, with a lower limit of detection of 0.032 μM.A real implementation of detecting Cu2+ in tap water was also carried out, and the results showed a satisfactory recovery rate. In a direct result of these, the implementation of a Cu2+ sensor may also be considered a real scenario.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Non-fluorescence of the complex between CQD and Cu2+ ions

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the detection and quantification for heavy metals in biological and ecological processes has garnered increasing interest because of their potentially devastating effects on humans. Copper is a third most prevalent heavy metal in human body and it was necessary for physiological processes, energy production, dioxygen transport, activation and signal transduction [1]. An excess of Cu2+ with in human body may lead to severe disorders including Alzheimer's disease, menkes disease, as well as damage to the liver or kidney and other symptoms [1].

Cu2+ ions may affect the human body via water polluted through consumer waste, industrial, heavy metals discharged into the environment as a result of acid rain-induced soil erosion. Because of the possible health hazards associated with Cu2+ consumption, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have determined the minimum allowed concentration of Cu2+ ions in drinking water at 1.3 ppm or 20 μM [2].

Presently, there is a great deal of interest in the production of novel nanomaterials, in addition to low-effort-based plans for the introduction of methods of analysis that are accurate and efficient for detecting the Cu2+ in this environment. Atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectrometry, potentiometric sensors and coupled plasma mass spectrometry are some of the standard analytical techniques that may be used to detect Cu2+ in a solution [3–6]. The efficiency of these analytical techniques for detection of Cu2+, meanwhile, usually demands expensive equipment, it consumes more time, include potentially toxic or hazardous chemicals, and require sophisticated sample preparation. In order to address over these drawbacks, researchers developed the straightforward method of preparation known as the stirrer assisted approach. This approach uses natural resources as a source of carbon and is used in the process of developing CQDs.

Fluorescent-based sensors are now recognized as the optimum effective tool for detecting the heavy metals. These sensors seek to achieve high sensitivity, selectivity, minimum interferences, cost-effectiveness, and a rapid response [7]. Fluorescence nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, were often utilized in fluorescent investigations. These investigations required the use of very hazardous precursors, numerous production and purification processes, and expensive chemicals enabling surface modification [8]. As a consequence of this, the highest significance to develop an innovative approach that is straightforward, rapid, and efficient in the production of fluorescence nanomaterials.

In this case, semiconductor quantum dots have been supplemented by carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are a sophisticated and fascinating form of carbon nanomaterial. This is because CQDs are water-soluble, have a lot of functional groups, are not toxic and have great physical and chemical properties [9]. In other words, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have obtained over for semiconductor quantum dots. Researchers have always been attracted by the possibility of developing an approach that is straightforward, economical, and kind to the natural environment in order to manufacture carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In the process of fabricating CQDs, carbonization of organic substrates and quantum confinement of carbon materials, including carbon nanotubes and graphene are often employed techniques. Green synthesis addresses the production of CQDs utilizing natural carbon sources, is tremendously significant since, in contrast to the confinement approach, it is straightforward, easy to reproduce, harmless to the environment, and cost-effective. Another reason for the significant interest in green CQD synthesis is the vast availability of natural carbon sources.

Using hydrothermal, oxidative, microwave irradiation, heating, and pyrolysis techniques, broad range of organic carbon sources from which CQDs were generated. The domestic waste of carbon included products including fruit peels, animal byproducts, bakery goods, human byproducts, kitchen waste, vegetables peels, spices, and plant leaves. These techniques were used to create the fluorescent CQDs. Surface passivation does not work, and the great majority of the chemical substances and catalysts employed in the process are very harmful to both people and the environment. Over the last several years, there has been a significant amount of interest in the heteroatom doping of CQDs. Metal and transition metal atoms, on the other hand, have larger radii than carbon atoms, which means that they reduce the effectiveness of doping and cause it to be less evenly dispersed throughout the material [10].

Turning biomass waste into carbon nanostructures has gained a striking attention in the recent years in the global materials research community. Biomass waste derived carbon are used for various applications like supercapacitors, sensors and pollutants absorbents. They are easy to prepare and moreover, adds value to a waste material. Among several possibilities, in this research we have explored the preparation and properties of CQDs prepared from rice stock. Around 92.9 T Tones of rice are produced in India and its market is also growing worldwide. The rice stock from rice are inconsumable and end up in food waste. Using rice stock as a source will help bring down the cost of preparation of biomass derived carbon nanomaterials. Moreover, from EDAX, it can be seen that the rice stock is mainly composed of Carbon and Oxygen alone, which makes it a reliable source for the production of P-CQDs (Fig. S.4). The goal of this study is to establish rice stock as a useful bio-waste that can be converted into valuable carbon quantum dots, which can then be used in the detection for Cu2+ ions.

In the process of this work, we developed a method for the production of P-CQDs that is efficient, non-toxic, and friendly to the environment. The carbon and phosphorus sources that we used were rice stock and phosphoric acid, respectively. The P-CQDs were characterized and the utilization of P-CQDs in a variety of metal ion detecting methods has been investigated and compared. For the most sensitive and specific detection of Cu2+ ions, P-CQDs have been employed only as fluorescent probes in all of the research that has been conducted. Fluorescent P-CQDs were unable to emit light because of the existence of Cu2+complexes, which were created when non-fluorescent P-CQDs were combined with Cu2+ ions. We used P-CQDs derived from rice stock and a method that was helped by a stirrer in order to employ a fluorescence-based sensor for the detection of Cu2+ ions. This sensor is non-toxic, inexpensive, is made from easily available rice stock, and is appropriate for use in large-scale operations. As a result of this, in the current study, we proposed astraightforward stirrer-assisted method for developing P-doped CQDs and we evaluate their application as fluorescent for Cu2+ ions sensing.

Rice was purchased for this investigation from a supermarket (Coimbatore, Tamilnadu, India). All the chemicals that were employed in this investigation were of an analytical grade and were purchased from Himedia and Sigma-Aldrich. They did not undergo any additional purification in the synthesis process and all used aqueous solutions, DI water, also known as deionized water were employed.

P-CQDs were produced from rice stock in a single step using a stirrer-assisted method ( Fig. 1). Rice stock was extracted from the strain of boiling rice, which was then diluted with DI water. The pH of rice stock was 6.2. To enhance the PL characteristics, the pH of the solution was made to 7 by adding NaOH. A combination containing 2.5 mL of rice stock, 2.5 mL of phosphoric acid, and 15 mL of distilled water was stirred continuously at a temperature of80°C. As a result of the stirring process, the sugar molecules were heated, which resulted in their dehydration, polymerization, and carbonation into nuclei, which led to the formation of amorphous P-CQDs. The solution transformed from transparent to possessing a light brown colour after being rapidly oxidized, and the light brown precipitate was filtered using Whatmann filter paper. Following the centrifugation of the solution around 12,500 rpm, its supernatant were filtered through themembrane filter of 0.22 µm and a dialysis membrane (3500 MWCO). The finished product were lyophilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation on morphological and structural analysis of P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Particle size distribution and surface morphology of a synthesized P-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2(a). The particles showed a spherical morphology. The histogram of P-CQDs particle diameter vs the area of particle size distribution obtained using Image J software is shown in Fig. 2(b). It was determined from the histogram shows that the size distribution for P-CQD particle sizes varied from 3 nm to 8 nm, having an average diameter of 5.4 nm. A particle size for P-CQDs was observed to be less than 10 nm, indicating that sample was in the quantum dot range and were in good agreement with prior information.

The hydrodynamic diameter of P-CQDs were calculated using the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) method (Fig. S.5). P-CQDs had hydrodynamic size distributions of 14.84 nm, which is slightly higher than the TEM observation. In general, the hydrodynamic diameter of P-CQDs in a solvent is higher than particle size measured in vacuum. The P-CQDs without functional groups on their surface can readily precipitate in aqueous solvents and the existence of several functional groups on the surface of P-CQDs can promote their dispersion in a water-based solvent; therefore, functionalization of P-CQDs is a prerequisite for long-term colloidal stability. The improved dispersion of P-CQDs in deionized water can be attributed with contributing to the smaller size that was determined by DLS.

XPS spectra were used in order to investigate both the chemical bonding and elemental composition of P-CQDs. According to the XPS spectra, which exhibited three distinct peaks at 132, 284, and 531 eV, the P-CQDs were mostly composed of carbon, phosphorous, and oxygen atoms ( Fig. 3(a)). The existence of a P(2p) peak was observed, and using Gaussian functions, it was deconvoluted to two peaks with 132.1 and 133.2 eV respectively (Fig. 3(b)). These peaks could been attributed as P–C and P–O, respectively. At 284.1, 285.9, and 288.1 eV, the investigation of three peaks in the C(1 s) spectrum indicates the existence of aromatic C–C and C–O/C–P (Fig. 3(c)). On the other hand, the O(1 s) have three peaks that correspond to CO and PO with respective energies of 531.0, 532.4, and 535.7 eV (Fig. 3(d)).

In XRD pattern, there were two noticeable diffraction peaks about 14.4° and 27.5° in the XRD spectra of P-doped CQDs ( Fig. 4(a)). These diffraction spectra are comparable to the typical peaks of graphite oxide. Specifically, in terms of its primary peak, P-doped CQDs had a diffraction peak of around 14.4° (d001 =0.613 nm) that was comparable to the standard graphite oxide diffraction peak (2θ =10.6° in the (001) plane). It was explained by an improvement in the intermediate distance between sp3 layers. During the process of carbonization, the functional groups were generated and these groups were attached to an edges of basal planes which form the P-CQDs structure. This led to an increase in the intermediate spacing that exists between the layers.

In addition, its secondary peak of P-CQDs at 27.5° (d002 =0.323 nm) was significantly stronger and similar to a typical graphite peak ((002) plane, 2θ = 26.5°) than graphite oxide. Although its upward shift (from 26.5° to 27.5°) was observed in comparison towards the ordered crystal structure of graphite, it has been associated to highly disordered carbon as well as reduction inside the distance between sp2 layers during the carbonization. An amorphous carbon structure of a P-CQDs was responsible for the reduction in the intensity of these peaks and its enhancement in full width half maximum.These findings indicate that an amorphous structure exists inside the carbon core of P-CQDs.

FT-IR spectra are utilized to investigate the various functional groups of P-CQDs (Fig. 4(b)). As revealed in P-CQDs, there are hydroxyl functional groups remaining in rice stock, which is why there was peak at 3325 cm−1. When a minor band at 2329 cm−1 was observed, it was determined to be the result of the formation of P-H bond. At 1689 cm−1, C-C stretching vibration may be responsible for P-CQDs, while a peak around 1479 cm−1 may be due to a C-O stretching vibration. In addition, two bands wereidentified at 1153 and 972 cm−1 that corresponded to the stretching vibrations of PO and P-O-C, respectively. It has been confirmed that phosphorus is doped with various compositions in combination with carbon-oxygen combination of P-CQDs. As an outcome, the FTIR data reveal that the surface of P-CQDs is functionalized with hydrophilic groups, which renders them extremely soluble in water.The zeta-potential of P-CQDs were observed at a sharp peak of − 56.7 mV (Fig. S.6). The negative potential indicates the presence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of P-CQDs. It is necessary for the surface of P-CQDs to have negative charge moieties which makes them highly soluble in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on optical properties of P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra for P-CQDs were investigated further to understand their optical properties (Fig. 4(d)). The optical absorption peak for P-CQDs reveal prominent peaks on 256 nm and the tail outspreading in that visible range centered at 487 nm, that corresponds to π-π * transition of a conjugated CC bond and the n-π * transition of CO bond, respectively.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of synthesized P-CQDs were utilizing a wide range of excitation wavelength ( Fig. 5(a)). The peak intensity for emission shifts depending on the wavelength of the associated excitation, which can be ranging from 310 to 430 nm; the maximum PL intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm with an emission wavelength of 535 nm. Even if the excitation wavelength shifts, the emission wavelength will not alter in a discernible way. The synthesized P-CQDs exhibited a photoluminescence (PL) emission behaviour that was independent of the excitation emission behaviour. The incorporation of phosphorus as a doping element is crucial for understanding the high fluorescence of CQDs. The doped atoms may cause a shift higher in the Fermi level. The phosphorous doping causes a red shift in fluorescence. The radiative recombination of excitons between the delocalized π orbital and the doped phosphorous atoms in P-CQDs results in a high PLQY of 32.61%, which is significantly more than the 16.47% in undoped CQDs. Phosphorous doping and surface functionalization have been observed to improve the quantum yield (QY) of CQD [11].

The influence of the ratio of the carbon source to the phosphoric acid on the photoluminescence (PL) of P-doped CQDs (Fig. 5(b)). The PL intensity of P-doped CQDs achieved their maximum intensity when the concentration of phosphorous source was equal to that of carbon source due to surface passivation. At room temperature, the aqueous solution of P-doped CQDs displayed a stable homogenous phase with no discernible precipitation.

In addition, the influence of pH on PL intensity for P-CQDs were investigated (Fig. 5(c)). When the pH was altered from 3 to 11, the PL intensity for P-CQDs remained unchanged. This suggests that PL intensity of P-CQDs in acid and alkali solutions changed only minimally. A possible explanation for this is that the surface of P-CQDs contains functional groups like –OH that are unstable at different pH levels, so that the protonation or deprotonation of these functional groupsonly affects their surface charge transfer and does not affect their core structure. This indicates that P-CQDs are not pH-dependent [12].

According to the relevant PL studies, the temperature had an impact on PL intensity (Fig. 5(d)). The fluorescence intensity increases, reaches a maximum at 60 °C, and then drops in PL intensity. In Fig. 5(d), the emission wavelength does not change in response to changes in temperature. This demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs remains unchanged throughout a wide range of temperatures. According to the findings of the investigation, P-CQDs can be used for a variety of applications in a multitude of environments. The P-CQD dispersion stability remained unchanged after one month, indicating that no precipitation or significant colourchange occurred in the solution (Fig. 5(e)). According to the PL spectra, there was no obvious agglomeration, and there was also no discernible decrease in the PL intensity [13].

The P-CQDs with the different metal ions (Mn3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Co2+, and Zn2+) that could be observed with the naked eye with concentrations at 260 µM to evaluate the selectivity and sensitivity of P-CQDs using UV light (365 nm) ( Fig. 6). The image seem to demonstrate the high selectivity and sensitivity for Cu2+ions. When compared to other metal ions, only Cu2+ ions diminished the PL intensity of P-CQDs. This analysis revealed that P-CQD has a strong affinity for Cu2+ ions.

In recent decades, the fluorescence sensors have been developed for the application of fluorometric detection of various metal ions, nitro aromatic compounds, antibiotics, vitamins etc. Here, the detection of metal ions due to the surface modification on the surface of P-CQDs that may form complexes with metal ions with non-radiative emission.The selectivity analysis of prepared P-CQDs solution was evaluated by PL experimentation with chelating metal ions (Pb2+, Mn3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Mg2+, Al3+and Zn2+) with a concentrations ranging from 0 to 260 µM [14].

The histogram was obtained based on the interaction of the quenching ratio (F/F0) and the presence of various metal ions, where F shows the presence of all other metal ions with a concentration of 260 µM and F0 shows the absence of all other metal ions with PL fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 7(c)). The concentration of P-CQDs was maintained constant throughout the experiments. The same conditions were used to create a blank control with no metal ions. In interference investigations, Cu2+ions and their combinations with all other metal ions were incorporated in P-CQD solution and their quenching on P-CQDs were evaluated. All metal ions have negligible influence on quenching with P-CQDs and this sensor is only sensitive to Cu2+ions. After analyzing the graph, we determined the Cu2+ ions had minimum quenching ratio and this shows the detection of Cu2+ions.

The sensitivity analysis of P-CQDs was assessed by the detection of Cu2+ions on P-CQDs at room temperature as part of the PL investigation (Fig. 7(a)). The PL intensity was recorded at 350 nm at room temperature. By adding the Cu2+ions on P-CQDs solution at a concentrations of 0–260 µM. The performance of proposed investigation reveals that the fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs decreases with the addition of Cu2+ions. This observations indicated that the "Turn-off fluorescence"occurs in the presence of Cu2+ions, as seen in Fig. 7. The PL intensity diminishes as the concentration of Cu2+ions increases. Cu2+ions may be detected simply, fast, sensitively, and selectively using our suggested approach. This work demonstrates that this PL fluorescence method for the application of fluorometric detection of Cu2+ions.

The plot illustrates that there was a linear relationship between the relative intensity [(F0-F)/F]at different P-CQD concentrations on PL intensity. Under ideal conditions, the linear relationship between the Cu2+concentrations and an efficiency of quenching. The relation between the fluorescence quenching intensity and the concentrations of Cu2+ is given by the equation (F0 - F)/F0 = 0.0069[Cu2+] (μM) - 6.4455 (R2 = 0.9932). The limit of detection of Cu2+ ions that could be found was 0.032 μM (formula (1)) where “σ”symbolizes the standard deviation of samples and“m” symbolizes the slope of a straight line. So, a Stern-Volmer plot with a perfect correlation coefficient for R2 was 0.9932with the Cu2+ions concentrations in Fig. 7(b). When comparing CQDs in this study to earlier reports, it can be noted that the sensing performance characteristics were comparable to those previously published CQDs ( Table 1).
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
m

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence mechanism of P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between phosphorous-doped carbon quantum dots and Cu2+ ions has an influence on the fluorescence quenching of phosphorous-doped carbon quantum dots. The mechanism for quenching may be divided into 2 distinct types: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Photo-induced electron transfer (PET), internal filtering effect (IFE), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) are the three primary mechanisms that might be involved in dynamic quenching.

The UV–vis spectra of Cu2+ ions demonstrate the presence of a significant absorption band located around 245 nm. According to this, it appears that this would not overlap with emission wavelength of 535 nm for P-CQDs ( Fig. 8(a)). This suggests that there is no observation of the FRET occurring while the quenching process takes place. There was no spectral overlapping between the absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of P-CQDs when examined employing IFE (Fig. 8(b))[21]. Because these two spectra do not overlap in any manner, the IFE has been neglected as a reasonable explanation.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra were analyzed in order to investigate the possible interactions that take place between P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions. The d-d transition of the solution containing Cu2+ ions was centered at 245 nm, the absorbance spectra of P-CQDs – Cu2+ revealed a red shift and extended band at 256 nm. The covalent binding effect between P-CQDs and Cu2+ ions was demonstrated by the red shift of the absorbance band of P-CQDs – Cu2+. Because electrons were transported from excited states of P-CQDs to unoccupied orbits of Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence quenching may be attributed to this process.There is no massive variation in the UV spectra of P-CQDs and P-CQDs + Cu2+ ions in the wavelength of 535 nm. It eliminates the quenching of PET and confirms the static quenching (Fig. 8(c)).

In order to get a better understanding of the process that explains the fluorescence quenching were explored the PL lifetime measurement for P-CQDs. In most cases, the linear Stern-Volmer relationships are associated with the presence of both dynamic and static quenching. In the case of dynamic quenching, the photoluminescence lifetimes of quantum dots change as a function of the concentration of Cu2+ions, but in the case of static quenching, their lifetimes remain constant (Fig. S.7). P-CQDs - Cu2+ions obtained a time resolved PL spectra of 7.61 ns, which was comparable to that of pure P-CQDs (7.63 ns). This suggests that the quenching mechanism may involve static quenching.

The detection of Cu2+ ions in tab water were utilized to evaluate the P-CQDs capability to sense in a real environment. The typical recovery procedures were performed on the municipal tap water. The PL spectra for real sample were evaluated with the standard addition technique at three different concentrations were evaluated with the standard addition technique at three different concentrations: low (50 μM, 70 μM), medium (120 μM, 140 μM) and high (240 μM, 260 μM) at pH 7 (Fig. S.8). Each sample was examined a total of three times (n = 3), always under the identical set of circumstances ( Table 2). The relative standard deviation is less than 5%, and the recovery rates were in the range of 94.42–99.9%, which demonstrates that the proposed P-CQDs may also be utilized for the purpose of the practical detection for Cu2+ ions in realistic specimens.

In conclusion, a stirrer-assisted technique for synthesizing fluorescent P-CQDs from rice stock as a carbon source that is sustainable and cost-effective has been proposed. As a result of biomass is so widely available, it may be used as a source of carbon precursor, and using this precursor becomes both cost-effective and devoid of negative impacts.The created P-CQDs had a spherical shape and a narrow size distribution of around 5.4 nm, with a quantum yield (QY) of 32.61%. When exposed to UV light, the synthesized P-CQDs emitted a green fluorescence. Under optimum circumstances, the interaction between the P-CQDs and Cu2+ions leads in the transfer of electrons, leading to the quenching of fluorescence. There was a linear correlation between fluorescence quenching and Cu2+ions concentration ranging from 0 to 260 μM, with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9932, and the detection limit was 0.032 μM. In addition, a practical application for the detection of Cu2+ions in water was performed, and the results demonstrated a remarkable recovery rate. Consequently, our study demonstrates the viability of employing rice stock derived P-doped CQDs as a Cu2+sensor.

M. Preethi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. N. Ponpandian: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. C. Viswanathan: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, DST-PURSE, UGC-SAP, Government of India for the instrumental facilities provided through these programs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129942.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
